n ecember  an rab peasant made an astonishing archeological discovery in pper gypt.
umors obscured the circumstances of this find--perhaps because the discovery was accidental,
and its sale on the black market illegal. or years even the identity of the discoverer remained
unknown. ne rumor held that he was a blood avenger another, that he had made the find near
the town of aj 'ammádì at the abal al-árif, a mountain honeycombed with more than 
caves. riginally natural, some of these caves were cut and painted and used as grave sites as
early as the sith dynasty, some , years ago.
hirty years later the discoverer himself, uhammad 'lí al-ammán told what happened.
hortly before he and his brothers avenged their father's murder in a blood feud, they had saddled
their camels and gone out to the abal to dig for   sabakh,   a soft soil they used to fertilize their
crops. igging around a massive boulder, they hit a red earthenware jar, almost a meter high.
uhammad 'lí hesitated to break the jar, considering that a   jinn , or spirit, might live inside. ut
realizing that it might also contain gold, he raised his mattock, smashed the jar, and discovered
inside thirteen papyrus books, bound in leather. eturning to his home in al-asr, uhammad
'll dumped the books and loose papyrus leaves on the straw piled on the ground net to the
oven. uhammad's mother, 'mm-hmad, admits that she burned much of the papyrus in the
oven along with the straw she used to kindle the fire.
 few weeks later, as uhammad 'lí tells it, he and his brothers avenged their father's death by
murdering hmed sma'il. heir mother had warned her sons to keep their mattocks sharp when
they learned that their father's enemy was nearby, the brothers seized the opportunity, "hacked off
his limbs . . . ripped out his heart, and devoured it among them, as the ultimate act of blood
revenge."
 -  -

earing that the police investigating the murder would search his house and discover the books,
uhammad 'lí asked the priest, al-ummus asiliyus bd al-asih, to keep one or more for
him. uring the time that uhammad 'lí and his brothers were being interrogated for murder,
aghib, a local history teacher, had seen one of the books, and suspected that it had value. aving
received one from al-ummus asiliyus, aghib sent it to a friend in airo to find out its worth.
old on the black market through antiquities dealers in airo, the manuscripts soon attracted the
attention of officials of the gyptian government. hrough circumstances of high drama, as we
shall see, they bought one and confiscated ten and a half of the thirteen leather-bound books,
called codices, and deposited them in the optic useum in airo. ut a large part of the
thirteenth code, containing five etraordinary tets, was smuggled out of gypt and offered for
sale in merica. ord of this code soon reached rofessor illes uispel, distinguished historian
of religion at trecht, in the etherlands. cited by the discovery, uispel urged the ung
oundation in urich to buy the code. ut discovering, when he succeeded, that some pages
were missing, he flew to gypt in the spring of  to try to find them in the optic useum.
rriving in airo, he went at once to the optic useum, borrowed photographs of some of the
tets, and hurried back to his hotel to decipher them. racing out the first line, uispel was
startled, then incredulous, to read "hese are the secret words which the living esus spoke, and
which the twin, udas homas, wrote down." uispel knew that his colleague .. uech, using
notes from another rench scholar, ean oresse, had identified the opening lines with fragments
of a reek   ospel of homas   discovered in the 's. ut the discovery of the whole tet raised
new questions id esus have a twin brother, as this tet implies ould the tet be an authentic
record of esus' sayings ccording to its title, it contained the   ospel ccording to homas   yet,
unlike the gospels of the ew estament, this tet identified itself as a   secret   gospel. uispel also
discovered that it contained many sayings known from the ew estament but these sayings,
placed in unfamiliar contets, suggested other dimensions of meaning. ther passages, uispel
found, differed entirely from any known hristian tradition the "living esus," for eample,
speaks in sayings as cryptic and compelling as en koans
esus said, "f you bring forth what is within you, what you bring forth will save you. f you do not
bring forth what is within you, what you do not bring forth will destroy you."
hat uispel held in his hand, the   ospel of homas,   was only one of the fifty-two tets
discovered at ag ammadi (the usual nglish transliteration of the town's name). ound into the
same volume with it is the   ospel of hilip,   which attributes to esus acts and sayings quite
different from those in the ew estament
. . . the companion of the avior is] ary agdalene. ut hrist loved] her more than all] the
disciples, and used to kiss her often] on her mouth]. he rest of the disciples were offended] . . .
hey said to him, "hy do you love her more than all of us" he avior answered and said to
them, "hy do  not love you as ( love) her"
ther sayings in this collection criticize common hristian beliefs, such as the virgin birth or the
bodily   resurrection,   as   naïve   misunderstandings.   ound   together   with   these   gospels   is   the
 pocryphon   (literally, "secret book")   of ohn,   which opens with an offer to reveal "the mysteries
and the] things hidden in silence" which esus taught to his disciple ohn.
uhammad 'lí later admitted that some of the tets were lost--burned up or thrown away. ut
what remains is astonishing some fifty-two tets from the early centuries of the hristian era--
including a collection of early hristian gospels, previously unknown. esides the   ospel of
homas   and the   ospel of hilip,   the find included the   ospel of ruth   and the   ospel to the
gyptians,   which identifies itself as "the sacred book] of the reat nvisible pirit]." nother
group of tets consists of writings attributed to esus' followers, such as the   ecret ook of ames,
 the   pocalypse of aul,   the   etter of eter to hilip,   and the   pocalypse of eter.
 hat   uhammad   'lí   discovered   at   ag   ammadi,   it   soon   became   clear,   were   optic
translations,   made   about   ,   years   ago,   of   still   more   ancient   manuscripts.   he   originals
themselves had been written in reek, the language of the ew estament as oresse, uech, and
uispel had recognized, part of one of them had been discovered by archeologists about fifty
 -  -

years earlier, when they found a few fragments of the original reek version of the   ospel of
homas.
 bout the dating of the manuscripts themselves there is little debate. amination of the datable
papyrus used to thicken the leather bindings, and of the optic script, place them c. .. -.
ut scholars sharply disagree about the dating of the original tets. ome of them can hardly be
later than c .   .. -, since renaeus, the orthodo ishop of yons, writing . , declares
that heretics "boast that they possess more gospels than there really are,'' and complains that in his
time such writings already have won wide circulation--from aul through ome, reece, and
sia inor.
uispel and his collaborators, who first published the   ospel of homas,   suggested the date of c.
..  for the original. ome reasoned that since these gospels were heretical, they must have
been written later than the gospels of the ew estament, which are dated c. -l l. ut recently
rofessor elmut oester of arvard niversity has suggested that the collection of sayings in the
 ospel of homas,   although compiled c. , may include some traditions even   older   than the
gospels of the ew estament, "possibly as early as the second half of the first century" (-)--
as early as, or earlier, than ark, atthew, uke, and ohn.
cholars investigating the ag ammadi find discovered that some of the tets tell the origin of
the human race in terms very different from the usual reading of enesis the   estimony of ruth,
 for eample, tells the story of the arden of den from the viewpoint of the serpent! ere the
serpent, long known to appear in nostic literature as the principle of divine wisdom, convinces
dam and ve to partake of knowledge while "the ord" threatens them with death, trying
jealously to prevent them from attaining knowledge, and epelling them from aradise when they
achieve it. nother tet, mysteriously entitled   he hunder, erfect ind,   offers an etraordinary
poem spoken in the voice of a feminine divine power
or  am the first and the last.  am the honored one and the scorned one.
 am the whore and the holy one.
 am the wife and the virgin....
 am the barren one, and many are her sons....
 am the silence that is incomprehensible....
 am the utterance of my name.
hese diverse tets range, then, from secret gospels, poems, and quasi-philosophic descriptions of
the origin of the universe, to myths, magic, and instructions for mystical practice.
hy were these tets buried-and why have they remained virtually unknown for nearly ,
years heir suppression as banned documents, and their burial on the cliff at ag ammadi, it
turns out, were both part of a struggle critical for the formation of early hristianity. he ag
ammadi tets, and others like them, which circulated at the beginning of the hristian era, were
denounced as heresy by orthodo hristians in the middle of the second century. e have long
known that many early followers of hrist were condemned by other hristians as heretics, but
nearly all we knew about them came from what their opponents wrote attacking them. ishop
renaeus,   who   supervised   the   church   in   yons,   c.   ,   wrote   five   volumes,   entitled   he
estruction and verthrow of alsely o-called nowledge,   which begin with his promise to set
forth the views of those who are now teaching heresy . . . to show how absurd and inconsistent
with the truth are their statements . . .  do this so that . . . you may urge all those with whom you
are connected to avoid such an abyss of madness and of blasphemy against hrist.
e denounces as especially "full of blasphemy" a famous gospel called the   ospel of   ruth. s
renaeus referring to the same   ospel of ruth   discovered at ag ammadi' uispel and his
collaborators, who first published the   ospel of ruth,   argued that he is one of their critics
maintains that the opening line (which begins "he gospel of truth") is not a title. ut renaeus
does   use   the   same   source   as   at   least   one   of   the   tets   discovered   at   ag   ammadi--the
 pocryphon   (ecret ook)   of ohn-- as ammunition for his own attack on such "heresy." ifty
years later ippolytus, a teacher in ome, wrote another massive   efutation of ll eresies   to
"epose and refute the wicked blasphemy of the heretics."
 -  -

his campaign against heresy involved an involuntary admission of its persuasive power yet the
bishops prevailed. y the time of the mperor onstantine's conversion, when hristianity
became an officially approved religion in the fourth   century, hristian   bishops, previously
victimized by the police, now commanded them. ossession of books denounced as heretical was
made a criminal offense. opies of such books were burned and destroyed. ut in pper gypt,
someone possibly a monk from a nearby monastery of t. achomius, took the banned books and
hid them from destruction--in the jar where they remained buried for almost , years.
ut those who wrote and circulated these tets did not regard   themselves   as "heretics. ost of the
writings use hristian terminology, unmistakable related to a ewish heritage. any claim to offer
traditions about esus that are secret, hidden from "the many" who constitute what, in the second
century, came to be called the "catholic church." hese hristians are now called gnostics, from the
reek word   gnosis,   usually translated as "knowledge." or as those who claim to know nothing
about ultimate reality are called agnostic (literally, "not knowing"), the person who does claim to
know such things is called gnostic ("knowing"). ut   gnosis is   not primarily rational knowledge.
he   reek   language   distinguishes   between   scientific   or   reflective   knowledge   ("e   knows
mathematics") and knowing through observation or eperience ("e knows me"), which   is gnosis.
 s the gnostics use the term, we could translate it as "insight," for   gnosis   involves an intuitive
process of knowing oneself. nd to know oneself, they claimed, is to know human nature and
human destiny. ccording to the gnostic teacher heodotus, writing in sia inor (c. -),
the gnostic is one has come to understand who we were, and what we have become where we
were... whither we are hastening from what we are being released what birth is, and what is
rebirth.
et to know oneself, at the deepest level, is simultaneously to know od this is the secret of
 gnosis.   nother gnostic teacher, onoimus, says
bandon the search for od and the creation and other matters of a similar sort. ook for him by
taking yourself as the starting point. earn who it is within you who makes everything his own
and says, "y od, my mind, my thought, my soul, my body." earn the sources of sorrow, joy,
love, hate . . . f you carefully investigate these matters you will find him   in yourself .
hat uhammad 'll discovered at ag ammadi is, apparently, a library of writings, almost all
of them gnostic. lthough they claim to offer secret teaching, many of these tets refer to the
criptures of the ld estament, and others to the letters of aul and the ew estament gospels.
any of them include the same   dramatic personae   as the ew estament--esus and his disciples.
et the differences are striking.
rthodo ews and hristians insist that a chasm separates humanity from ts creator od is
wholly other. ut some of the gnostics who wrote these gospels contradict this self-knowledge is
knowledge of od the self and the divine are identical.
econd, the "living esus" of these tets speaks of illusion and enlightenment, not of sin and
repentance, like the esus of   the ew estament. nstead of coming to save us from sin, he comes
as   a   guide   who   opens   access   to   spiritual   understanding.   ut   when   the   disciple   attains
enlightenment, esus no longer serves as his spiritual master the two have become equal--even
identical.
hird, orthodo hristians believe that esus is ord and on of od in a unique way he remains
forever distinct from the rest of humanity whom he came to save. et the gnostic   ospel of
homas   relates that as soon as homas recognizes him, esus says to homas that they have both
received their being from the same source
esus said, " am not your master. ecause you have drunk, you have become drunk from the
bubbling stream which  have measured out.... e who will drink from my mouth will become as 
am  myself shall become he, and the things that are hidden will be revealed to him."
oes not such teaching--the identity of the divine and human. the concern with illusion and
enlightenment, the founder who is presented not as ord, but as spiritual guide sound more
astern than estern ome scholars have suggested that if the names were changed, the "living
 -  -

uddha" appropriately could say what the   ospel of homas   attributes to the living esus. ould
indu or uddhist tradition have influenced gnosticism
he ritish   scholar of uddhism,   dward onze, suggests that it had. e points out that
"uddhists were in contact with the homas hristians (that is, hristians who knew and used
such writings as the   ospel of homas)   in outh ndia." rade routes between the reco-oman
world and the ar ast were opening up at the time when gnosticism flourished (.. -) for
generations, uddhist missionaries had been proselytizing in leandria. e note, too, that
ippolytus,   who   was   a   reek   speaking   hristian   in   ome   (c.   ),   knows   of   the   ndian
rahmins--and includes their tradition among the sources of heresy
here is . . . among the ndians a heresy of those who philosophize among the rahmins, who live
a self-sufficient life, abstaining from (eating) living creatures and all cooked food . . . hey say that
od is light, not like the light one sees, nor like the sun nor fire, but to them od is discourse, not
that which finds epression in articulate sounds, but that of knowledge   (gnosis)   through which
the secret mysteries of nature are perceived by the wise.
ould the title of the   ospel of homas-- named for the disciple who, tradition tells us, went to
ndia--suggest the influence of ndian tradition
hese hints indicate the possibility, yet our evidence is not conclusive. ince parallel traditions
may emerge in different cultures at different times, such ideas could have developed in both
places independently. hat we call astern and estern religions, and tend to regard as separate
streams, were not clearly differentiated , years ago. esearch on the ag ammadi tets is
only   beginning   we   look   forward   to   the   work   of   scholars   who   can   study   these   traditions
comparatively to discover whether they can, in fact, be traced to ndian sources.
ven so, ideas that we associate with astern religions emerged in the first century through the
gnostic movement in the est, but they were suppressed and condemned by polemicists like
renaeus.   et   those   who   called   gnosticism   heresy   were   adopting--consciously   or   not--the
viewpoint of that group of hristians who called themselves orthodo hristians.  heretic may
be anyone whose outlook someone else dislikes or denounces. ccording to tradition, a heretic is
one who deviates from the true faith. ut what defines that "true faith" ho calls it that, and for
what reasons
e find this problem familiar in our own eperience. he term "hristianity," especially since the
eformation, has covered an astonishing range of groups. hose claiming to represent "true
hristianity" in the twentieth century can range from a atholic cardinal in the atican to an
frican ethodist piscopal preacher initiating revival in etroit, a ormon missionary in
hailand, or the member of a village church on the coast of reece. et atholics, rotestants, and
rthodo agree that   such diversity   is a recent--and deplorable--development. ccording to
hristian legend, the early church was different. hristians of every persuasion look back to the
primitive church to find a simpler, purer form of hristian faith. n the apostles' time, all members
of the hristian community shared their money and property all believed the same teaching, and
worshipped together all revered the authority of the apostles. t was only after that golden age
that conflict, then heresy emerged so says the author of the cts of the postles, who identifies
himself as the first historian of hristianity.
ut the discoveries at ag ammadi have upset this picture. f we admit that some of these fifty-
two tets represents early forms of hristian teaching, we may have to recognize that early
hristianity   is   far   more   diverse   than   nearly   anyone   epected   before   the   ag   ammadi
discoveries.
ontemporary   hristianity,   diverse   and   comple   as   we   find   it,   actually   may   show   more
unanimity than the hristian churches of the first and second centuries. or nearly all hristians
since that time, atholics, rotestants, or rthodo, have shared three basic premises. irst, they
accept the canon of the ew estament second, they confess the apostolic creed and third, they
affirm specific forms of church institution. ut every one of these-the canon of cripture, the creed,
and the institutional structure--emerged in its present form only toward the end of the second
century. efore that time, as renaeus and others attest, numerous gospels circulated among
 -  -

various hristian groups, ranging from those of the ew estament, atthew, ark, uke, and
ohn, to such writings as the   ospel of homas,   the   ospel of hilip,   and the   ospel of ruth,
 as well as many other secret teachings, myths, and poems attributed to esus or his disciples. ome
of these, apparently, were discovered at ag ammadi many others are lost to us. hose who
identified themselves as hristians entertained many--and radically differing-religious beliefs and
practices. nd the communities scattered throughout the known world organized themselves in
ways that differed widely from one group to another.
et by . . , the situation had changed. hristianity had become an institution headed by a
three-rank hierarchy of bishops, priests, and deacons, who understood themselves to be the
guardians of the only "true faith." he majority of churches, among which the church of ome
took a leading role, rejected all other viewpoints as heresy. eploring the diversity of the earlier
movement, ishop renaeus and his followers insisted that there could be only one church, and
outside of that church, he declared, "there is no salvation." embers of this church alone are
orthodo (literally, "straight-thinking") hristians. nd, he claimed, this church must be   catholic--
 that is, universal. hoever challenged that consensus, arguing instead for other forms of hristian
teaching, was declared to be a heretic, and epelled. hen the orthodo gained military support,
sometime after the mperor onstantine became hristian in the fourth century, the penalty for
heresy escalated.
 he ag ammadi discovery of manuscripts
 n ecember , two peasants, uhammed and halifah 'li of the al-amman clan were
digging for fertiliser at the base of the abal al-arif cliff, using the saddle-bags of their camels to
carry the earth back.   he cliff is about km north-east of ag ammadi. hey tethered their
camels to a boulder, and came upon a buried jar as they were digging around the base of the
boulder.   uhammed 'li told ..obinson that at first he was afraid to break the jar -- the lid
may have been sealed with bitumen, as a blackish substance is present on the lid -- for fear a jinn
might be inside, but the thought that gold might be contained instead, he broke it with his
mattock. ut flew particles of papyrus. he jar was of red slip ware, with four small handles near
the opening.   t was large, approimately cm or more in height, with an opening of some -
cm widening to cm in the side. he jar had been closed by fitting a bowl into its mouth. he
bowl survives, and is optic red slip ware of the -th century, with a rim decorated with four
fields of stripes.   he diameter at the outer edge is .-. cm, with inside diameter of .-
.cm.
he books were divided among the  camel-drivers present. ccording to 'li there were  (our
'code ' was not included in this number, as it was inside code ).   hus a code was lost
more or less at the site. even lots were drawn up. overs were removed and each consisted of a
complete code plus part of another. he other drivers, ignorant of the value and afraid of sorcery
and uhammed 'li, disclaimed any share, whereon he piled them all back together.
'li wrapped his books in his tunic and took them home, to his hovel in the hamlet of al-asr,
built on the site of ancient henoboskion. he books, loose covers and loose pages were dumped
in the straw, net to the oven.  blood-feud was in progress, for which reason uhammed 'li
was very careful not to venture back later to the area.   uhammed deposited the books with a
local coptic priest, asiliyus 'bd al-asih, as the police were searching his house almost nightly
for weapons. he priest's wife had a brother, aghib ndrawus, who went from village to village
teaching nglish and history in the local coptic church schools. e came to visit, and, on seeing
one of the books, recognised it might be valuable and took it to airo.   here he showed it to a
optic physician interested in the optic language, eorge obhi, who called in the authorities
from the epartment of ntiquities. hey seized the book, agreeing to pay aghib £ . fter
endless haggling, he got £ , on condition he donated the remaining £  to the optic
useum. he book was deposited in the museum, according to the register, on th ctober .
hinking the books were worthless, or maybe even unlucky to have, 'li's widowed mother 'mm
hmad had burned part of those lying in the straw in the oven (probably the covers and most of

he ag ammadi ibrary
 hat is nosticism
 nosis   and   gnosticism   are still rather arcane terms, though in the last two decades the words have
been increasingly encountered in the vocabulary of contemporary society.   nosis   derives from
reek, and connotes "knowledge" or the "act of knowing". (n first hearing, it is sometimes
confused with another more common term of the same root but opposite sense   agnostic , literally
"not   knowing",   a   knower   of   nothing.)   he   reek   language   differentiates   between   rational,
propositional knowledge, and the distinct form of knowing obtained not by reason, but by
personal eperience or perception. t is this latter knowledge, gained from eperience, from an
interior spark of comprehension, that constitutes   gnosis .   
 n the first century of the hristian era this term,   nostic,   began to be used to denote a prominent,
even if somewhat heterodo, segment of the diverse new hristian community. mong these
early followers of hrist, it appears that an elite group delineated themselves from the greater
household of the hurch by claiming not simply a belief in hrist and his message, but a "special
witness" or revelatory eperience of the divine. t was this eperience, this   gnosis,   which--so these
nostics claimed--set the true follower of hrist apart from his fellows. tephan oeller eplains
that these nostic hristians held a "conviction that direct, personal and absolute knowledge of
the authentic truths of eistence is accessible to human beings, and, moreover, that the attainment
of such knowledge must always constitute the supreme achievement of human life."   
 hat the "authentic truths of eistence" affirmed by the nostics were will be briefly reviewed
below. ut a historical overview of the early hurch might first be useful. n the initial decades of
the   hristian   church--the   period   when   we   find   first   mention   of   "nostic"   hristians--no
orthodoy, or single acceptable format of hristian thought, had yet been defined. uring this
first century of hristianity modern scholarship suggests nosticism was one of many currents
sweeping the deep waters of the new religion. he ultimate course hristianity, and estern
culture with it, would take was undecided at that early moment nosticism was one of forces
forming that destiny.

 hunder, erfect ind   ( ,) presents the revelation discourse of a female divinity who speaks alternately in first-
person statements of identity (" ") and second-person address. he tet's parallelism of structure, together with its
etensive use of antithesis, parado, and other literary devices, point clearly to its poetic or hymnic character. . ayton
has argued persuasively that the paradoical and often outrageous pairing of antithetical terms in the " " statements
 -        -

of hunder can be read as a comple identity riddle to be solved by the knowing or "gnostic" reader. t the same time,
attention to various features of the tet as a whole suggests that it is not only the mystery of the speaker's identity, but
the relationship between the divine speaker and her human hearers that forms the eegetical cru of the tet.
hunder focuses attention on the hearers' relationship to the divine speaker not only through its alternating structure of
first-person proclamation and second-person address, but also through its metaphorical imagery of kinship and gender,
its references to the audience's responses to the divine, and its claims about the speaker's role in the operations of
language and intellect. ts persistent, uncompromising use of parado pushes its hearers to relinquish the apparent
sense of its words and to seek the hidden meaning of individual utterances and of the discourse as a whole. inally, by
locating the divine in the "voice" and "hearing" of the tet, it leads its hearers or readers to find the divine within the tet
and within themselves, and so to discover themselves within the divine. n such an interpretive movement of letting go
and finding, of becoming sober and being found, the tet's final words suggest, the reader "goes up" to the salvific "place
of rest," "finds" the divine persona revealed in the tet, and "enters into" a state of living and not dying again.
hunder's conception of salvation does not conform to the more familiar "nostic" image of a transformative movement
or `conversion' from blindness to vision, deficiency to fullness, or ignorance to gnosis. ather, hunder presents an
understanding of salvation that comes through the interpretive process of grappling with the language of the tet and
confronting the paradoical nature of the divine within the antitheses of ignorance and gnosis, weakness and power,
shame and honor, death and life. t the same time, hunder forges a sharp distinction between those who merely hear
the words of the tet and those who hear the divine voice with gnosis, that is, between those who remain ignorant and
those who come to know the divine in the fullness of her compleity and mystery. hose who hear the utterances of
hunder with gnosis enter into salvific relation with the divine through the interchange of divine utterance and divine
hearing, manifested within the symbolic world of the tet and within themselves. "hunder, erfect ind" may thus be
interpreted not only as a title for the tet, but as a name for the divine speaker, her thundering utterances, and her place
of dwelling within those who hear her voice and know her mystery with the salvific gnosis and hearing of perfect mind.
 etting in ncient editerranean eligions
 hile hunder resembles many other ancient tets in various ways, its distinctive combination of features is virtually
unmatched in the religious and philosophical literature of antiquity. n form, hunder's first-person proclamations of
identity (" ") parallel most directly the aretalogies of the goddess sis. et the tet's alternation between first-person
identity statements and second-person address bring it closer to the form of the philosophical sermon or diatribe,
familiar from the monologues of biblical isdom (e.g., rov , ir , is -,  noch ). n addition, hunder
combines these literary modes with rhetorical features, such as parado and antithesis, which are more characteristic of
the reek riddle.
n content as well as form, the closest parallels to hunder are found in the hymnic speeches of female divinities in other
ag ammadi tets, especially those of ronoia (in p. ohn ,,-,) rotennoia (throughout rim. rot. ,)
ophia oe, the "ve of ife" (in rig. orld ,,-) and the spiritual ve in he ospel of ve (piph., an.
..). et unlike most of these divine discourses, the utterances of hunder have no narrative or epistolary setting.
hey appear as unmediated divine speech, addressed directly from the divine to her human hearers or readers.
here is no general scholarly consensus on the social or historical setting of hunder. hile scholars agree that the tet
ehibits no eplicitly ewish or hristian elements, its imagery resonates with a variety of sources, including ewish and
hristian isdom, sis traditions, iddle latonism, toicism, and with other ag ammadi tets, especially those
designated "ethian" and "alentinian." hunder may profitably be read in relation to all of these tets and traditions as
a revisionary poetic work that puts forward a distinctive perspective on the nature of the divine and her relation to
humankind.

 hunder, erfect ind   eists only in the optic version found at ag ammadi ( ,,-,). he author, date,
and place of composition are unknown, but a cultural milieu like that of second- or third-century leandria is
plausible. n any case, it is clear that the tet was originally composed in reek well before  .., the approimate
date of the optic manuscript. he surviving tet is relatively well preserved, with minor damage near the top of the
first four pages (-) and somewhat more serious damage on the first ten lines of the remaining manuscript pages (-
).

    nnoia   is the reek word for thought.
    ous   is the reek word for ind. n ermetic and nostic writings, ous often is used as a name or a description of the one true od.
    ogos   is the reek word often translated into nglish as ord. n the original reek it is more of a reference to ratio, order, or logic, although language (hence
'word') is also a valid choice. ogos, in hristological writings is usually assumed to refer to hrist. his assumption, however, is drawn into question when one
observes the use of the word in the ermetic and non-hristian nostic traditions, as "he hought of orea" is an eample of. t should be read as the saving
logic, or knowledge (gnosis) by which salvation is achieved.
    his part begins the hought with an address to higher beings, formed as an invocation. lthough earson details it as being to the divine ethian triad of ather,
other and on, this does not seem so clear. learly more than one being is addressed, as orea refers to "them" in line , and clearly the on is addressed
("ogos," line ) and the ather ("ous," line ), but presumably the nnoia and/or the oice is meant to refer to the divine other.
    orea , under many variations of spelling, is a frequent figure in nostic literature (cf. especially he ypostasis of the rchons), under a great variety of
circumstances, from the ark burning wife of oah to the sister-wife of eth. n this last capacity, in ethian nosticism, she often appears as a female equivalent of
his male savior figure. ere, however, she takes the position of savior alone.
    egins the second segment in the hought, where orea is taken from this world into the divine world (the leroma), being her proper place. t is telling in that the
higher powers do not receive her into their place, but into hers. he is brought into the thought of the mind, and there can speak with all the mperishable nes,
being the higher powers themselves and all the saved. e see that she has inherited the ogos, which a hristian would read as receiving the pirit of the ord,
hrist, and she also becomes utogenetic. hus she, so to speak, is taken into the odhead.
    dentified as "the ather of the ll" and "the ather of ous" (or mind),   damas   is a name often used to refer to the father of all mankind. ne will notice, though,
that, unlike the ringhamite ormons, this does not imply that a man, dam, is the father of all, or the father of od (ous), for damas is present in all that dams
(see note, line ). his is, instead, a transcendent being, a totality of thoughts, by which salvation is achieved, roughly equivalent to the hristian oly pirit.
    pinoia  lthough this annotator does not know from what reek word this is derived, it evidently refers to the leroma once again, wherein orea may find rest.
    irst mind  refers once again to the ous, being the first mind of od. he significance is that it points out once again that she has inherited the very mind of od.
    utogenes   is the reek word meaning "self-begotten," in that the reator is self-creating, and needs no eternal force to bring im into being. n ermetic and
nostic writings it is often used as an alternate name for the one true od. he significance here is that, having been freed from the bonds of this corruptible world,
orea becomes "self-begotten," able to generate herself, showing the immortality of the soul and the fact that the appearance of creation is misleading.
    iving ogos  in nostic tets, the term "iving hrist", as opposed to simply "hrist," is used to refer to the secret or spiritual being, as opposed to the earthly or
corruptible being. he use here may be construed as to refer to the immortal and spiritual ogos (note ), as opposed to the ogos that manifests itself in the world
which we may perceive. orea has, thus, inherited the true knowledge of reality, not simply earthly knowledge as can be studied from eperiences here on this
plane.
    art three he has here received the very mind of od into herself, and simultaneously sees and is the odhead, or leroma.
    ords of ife  ere, earson conjectures that the words of ife refer to the capacity to deliver salvation, and thus shows that orea achieves the capacity to save
mankind by virtue of her salvation.
    "that which she had received before the world came into being" refers to the immortality of the soul, and the concept that the soul, although it may believe or
perceive itself to be apart from the leroma, in reality has received the mind of od (note ), the living ogos, and the words of ife before this corruptible world
came into being.
    leroma    he leroma is the word referring to the thirty highest eons, constituting the one true od, although it also refers to the beings that occupy those
eons. his is how orea may simultaneously see and be the leroma. ssentially, this word means what is thought of as the odhead in astern culture.
    ourth and final section of the hought, wherein it speaks of orea's salvation, and how it is aided.
    eficiency  he concept of being in or out of deficiency is a common one in nostic writings, and refers to being filled or not filled with the spirit of od (or,
among hristian nostics, hrist). ne who is not filled with the spirit of od is deficient, and thirsts, and is not saved. he orea perceives the leroma and is not
deficient demonstrates that being one with the odhead is salvation, and fills one with the spirit.
    our holy helpers  earson identifies these helpers with the four "luminaries" of ethian nosticism, armozel, roiael, aveithe, and leleth. hese beings are
more detailed in elchizedek, where it is seen that they are referred to as "commander" or "ommander-in-chief," and seem to fulfill a position somewhat similar to
the rchangels in hristianty, although elchizedek groups the "offspring of the rchons" with the offspring of "all the angels," and so the luminaries may be of a
higher type. ere they are seen as entities aiding by intercession in orea's continual salvation, thus implying that salvation is something that can be lost.
    dams  dam being the ebrew for "man," should be read as meaning the reference here to the dams as the mass of created humanity. hat damas   is
present in all dams shows that the transcendent totality of thoughts is present in all created mankind.
